attributes.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of progressive dyspnoea and right-sided chest pain. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She has not had any recent surgery, immobilisation, or long-distance travel. On examination, she appears mildly distressed. Her pulse rate is 105/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Chest examination reveals reduced air entry at the right base. CT pulmonary angiogram confirms bilateral pulmonary emboli. Anticoagulation has been commenced.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following investigations should be performed to investigate the cause of the pulmonary embolism?
options: Factor V Leiden and prothrombin gene mutation
CT scan of abdomen and pelvis
Antithrombin, protein C, and protein S
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with an unprovoked pulmonary embolism at an older age. Hence, occult malignancy should be considered. Age-appropriate cancer screening (including mammogram, cervical screening, and bowel cancer screening) is recommended as the initial approach.

Routine thrombophilia testing, including Factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, antithrombin, protein C, and protein S should not be performed in older patients. According to the 2019 guidelines from the Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand, younger patients (<45 years) with an unprovoked proximal DVT and PE may undergo testing for antithrombin and protein C and S deficiency if it may change anticoagulation duration. Patients should receive appropriate counselling regarding the implications before testing, with specialist advice taken too.

CT scan of abdomen and pelvis as part of an extensive cancer screening protocol has not been shown to improve outcomes compared to age-appropriate screening alone. The SOME trial demonstrated that extensive screening with CT does not significantly impact cancer-related mortality in patients with unprovoked VTE.

Bilateral lower limb ultrasound is not routinely indicated when pulmonary embolism has already been confirmed, unless there are specific symptoms suggesting DVT that would change management. The presence or absence of DVT would not alter the treatment course in this case.
learningPoints: Age-appropriate cancer screening is recommended for patients with unprovoked proximal DVT and PE.
CT scan of abdomen and pelvis as part of an extensive cancer screening protocol has not been shown to improve outcomes compared to age-appropriate screening alone.
Thrombophilia testing is not routinely indicated in older patients with unprovoked venous thromboembolism.
references: Carrier M, Lazo-Langner A, Shivakumar S, Tagalakis V, Zarychanski R, Solymoss S, et al. Screening for Occult Cancer in Unprovoked Venous Thromboembolism. New England Journal of Medicine. 2015 Aug 20;373(8):697–704.  Available from: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1506623

Tran HA, Gibbs H, Merriman E, et al. New guidelines from the Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand for the diagnosis and management of venous thromboembolism [published correction appears in Med J Aust. 2019 Jul;211(2):94. doi: 10.5694/mja2.50260] [published correction appears in Med J Aust. 2020 Feb;212(3):108. doi: 10.5694/mja2.50486]. Med J Aust. 2019;210(5):227-235. Available from: https://www.mja.com.au/journal/2019/210/5/new-guidelines-thrombosis-and-haemostasis-society-australia-and-new-zealand

Ho WK, Hankey GJ, Eikelboom JW. Should adult patients be routinely tested for heritable thrombophilia after an episode of venous thromboembolism?. Med J Aust. 2011;195(3):139-142. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21806532/
